items.q: Most movements in the human body use which class of lever?
items.why: Students assume the most familiar (first class - seesaw) is most common. Actually most body levers are third class (effort between fulcrum and load) - like the biceps curl. They sacrifice mechanical advantage for speed/range.

items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.q: What is the rough formula for maximum heart rate?
items.why: Students misremember the formula. 220 - age is the classic estimate (rough; varies individually). A 20-year-old's estimated max HR is around 200 bpm; an 80-year-old's is around 140.
It's Force × time of application. “Mass × velocity” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Newton's third law is invoked when:
items.correct: A sprinter pushes back on the blocks and the blocks push forward
items.misconception: A ball decelerates due to gravity
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A sprinter pushes back on the blocks and the blocks push forward. “A ball decelerates due to gravity” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A 0.5 kg ball is hit changing velocity from 10 to 30 m s⁻¹. Impulse (N s) is?
items.why: Exact answer: 10. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
